The Long Version

(Feel free to skip this read if you are uninterested. I just want this to be written down so I don’t forget these details.)

The current version of record tags tracks two types of actions - destroyed and touched. A record is said to be touched if it has been created or updated. The main motivation behind tracking creates and updates as touched was simplicity. Our generated update seeds cover the create and updated cases rather well, therefore it didn’t seem necessary to make a distinction between them. In fact, under the new scheme we haven’t changed the seed generator much at all. Updates and created are still seeded together, and using the same general seeding pattern.

This change was prompted by a simple scenario. Someone makes a new record, then destroys it. Under the old scheme, we would first have a touched tag, then when the record was destroyed, we would remove the touched tag and replace it with a destroyed tag. One would very reasonably think that creating something then destroying it should give us a net of zero changes to seed. However, we are left with this leftover destroyed tag. Although maybe not ideal, we weren’t troubled by this because our seed generator will simply do a find_by then a try(:destroy), so if the record wasn’t there - nothing would happen.

Now the complicated stuff. This works in most cases, except if the record being destroyed has a foreign key in it’s candidate key. Consider the following example. Design makes a new MultiSet and adds a bunch of MultiSetMembers to it. Then, for whatever reason they decide to destroy the MultiSet. This leaves us with a bunch of destroyed tags, as described above. When the MultiSetMember seeds are ran, the to-be-seeded environment doesn’t have the MultiSet, which makes the MultiSetMember unidentifiable, and causes us to error out (enter a hair pulling afternoon of trying to retroactively figure out what went wrong).

There was talk of adding more code to our seeds to check for these kinds of scenarios and skip the record if this kind of case was present, but the last thing we need is even larger seeds, and a bunch of sketchy logic operating on ActiveRecord reflections to attempt to isolate these situations. Instead, we really should just not be generating any code for these records - again, our net change is zero, so let’s not seed anything.

Enter the hard earned realization distinguishing between updates and creates is actually a really good idea. Using the three states - created, updated, and destroyed, we can react intelligently to changes as they happen, and detect scenarios like - this designer is now destroying something they just created, so let’s not track it at all. As it turns out, we can make some other helpful choices given incoming tags vs. existing tags. Check this stuff out.

As you can see, this decision tree of sorts allows us to raise exceptions when we reach a state that should never happen. For example, if we ever see the same record being created twice then we know that somehow the tracking missed something. Either somewhere we’re using an INSERT statement or RecordTags was simply disabled while changes were made. Either way, this batch of seeds is going to have a problem, and it’s much better for us to be notified of that immediately, rather than having to reconstruct what went wrong after the fact.

Anyways, here’s to hoping that we can one day just forget about this system and let it quietly do it’s job without issue. :beers:
